intro: Let's do a final sweep of common deductions that apply to most Canadians — charitable donations, medical expenses, carrying charges, and union dues. These are frequently missed or under-claimed.

screens.title: Did any of the following apply to you in 2025?
screens.instruction: Select all that apply. We will only ask for details on what is relevant to you.

options.label: Made donations to registered Canadian charities
Paid medical expenses for yourself, spouse, or dependents
Paid interest on an investment loan or investment management fees
Paid spousal support under a court order or written agreement

screens.title: Did you make charitable donations to registered Canadian charities in 2025?
screens.instruction: Donations to registered Canadian charities are eligible for a non-refundable federal credit under ITA s.118.1 — 15% on the first $200 and 29% on amounts above $200 (33% if your income is above the top federal bracket).

true.message: Charitable donation receipts must be from registered Canadian charities. The federal credit is 15% on the first $200 and 29% on amounts above $200 under ITA s.118.1. Spouses can combine donations on one return to maximize the above-$200 rate. Unused donations can be carried forward up to 5 years. First-time donors (no donation claimed in the prior 5 years) qualify for a 25% First-Time Donor Super Credit on up to $1,000 in cash donations.

document_checklist_add.document: Official charitable donation receipts from registered Canadian charities — showing charity name, registration number, and donation amount

screens.title: Did you pay medical expenses for yourself, your spouse, or your dependents in 2025?
screens.instruction: Medical expenses are claimable under ITA s.118.2 as a non-refundable credit on amounts above 3% of net income (or $2,635, whichever is less). The best 12-month period ending in 2025 or 2026 can be used — it does not have to match the calendar year.

true.message: Medical expenses under ITA s.118.2(2) cover a wide range — prescriptions, dental, vision, physiotherapy, ambulance, home modifications for disability, private health insurance premiums, and many other items. The eligible list is extensive. Only amounts above 3% of your net income (minimum $2,635 in 2025) generate a credit. Pooling all family medical expenses on the lower-income spouse's return often maximizes the claim.

document_checklist_add.document: All medical expense receipts for the best 12-month period ending in 2025 or early 2026 — prescriptions, dental, vision, paramedical services, and private insurance premiums

preparer_flag.flag: Determine best 12-month period for medical expense claim — can span two calendar years. Pool all family expenses on lower-income spouse's return.

screens.title: Did you pay interest on money borrowed to earn investment income, or pay investment management fees in 2025?
screens.instruction: Carrying charges and interest on money borrowed to earn income are deductible under ITA s.20(1)(c) and s.20(1)(bb). This includes interest on investment loans and fees paid for investment advice on taxable accounts.

true.message: Interest on money borrowed to earn investment income is deductible under ITA s.20(1)(c) — for example, a loan used to purchase stocks, bonds, or a rental property. Investment management fees on taxable accounts (not RRSPs, TFSAs, or RRIFs) are deductible under ITA s.20(1)(bb). Fees on registered accounts are not deductible.

document_checklist_add.document: Investment loan interest statements and investment management fee receipts for taxable accounts only

screens.title: Did you pay spousal support under a court order or written agreement in 2025 that has not already been captured?
screens.instruction: This is a catch-all for taxpayers who did not go through the Marital Status branch but still make support payments.

options.label: Yes — I made spousal support payments in 2025

true.message: Spousal support paid periodically pursuant to a court order or written agreement is deductible under ITA s.60(b). Lump sum payments are generally not deductible. Bring your agreement and proof of payments made.

document_checklist_add.document: Separation agreement or court order and records of all spousal support payments made in 2025

screens.title: Do you have your 2024 Notice of Assessment available?
screens.instruction: Your 2024 Notice of Assessment confirms RRSP room, tuition and donation carryforwards, capital loss carryforwards, and other amounts that may affect your 2025 return.

options.label: No — I do not have it

true.message: Your 2024 NOA is a key document. Bring it to your preparer — it confirms your RRSP deduction limit, any carryforward balances, and your account balance with CRA.

document_checklist_add.document: 2024 Notice of Assessment — confirms RRSP room, donation carryforward, tuition carryforward, and capital loss carryforward

false.message: Your 2024 Notice of Assessment is available through CRA My Account. If you do not have access your preparer can retrieve it through CRA's Represent a Client portal. It is an important document — try to obtain it before your appointment.

document_checklist_add.document: 2024 Notice of Assessment — retrieve from CRA My Account or ask preparer to access via Represent a Client

highlighted_documents: Official charitable donation receipts from registered charities
All medical expense receipts for the best 12-month period
Investment loan interest statements and investment management fee receipts
branch_completion_summary.transition: Next: Credits Review — the final section.
